prompt.question: Which of the following Spring bean scopes is described as having a lifetime that matches a single HTTP request?
feedbacks: Incorrect, the session scope means that a bean instance is created and lives for the duration of an HTTP session. It is not limited to a single HTTP request.
Incorrect, the application scope means that a bean instance is shared across the entire ServletContext. It is not tied to a single HTTP request.
Incorrect, the global session scope is used in portlet-based applications and is not applicable to standard HTTP requests. It is not tied to a single HTTP request.

Correct, the request scope means that a new bean instance is created for each HTTP request and is destroyed once the request is complete. This scope is commonly used for beans that are specific to a single request, such as request parameters or temporary data.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring Framework, the scope of a bean determines its lifecycle and visibility. The request scope is one of the built-in scopes provided by Spring and is specific to web applications.

Key Features of the request Scope:

  1. Lifetime:

    • A bean with the request scope is created for each HTTP request.

    • It is destroyed once the request is completed.

  2. Use Cases:

    • Suitable for storing data that is specific to a single HTTP request, such as request parameters or temporary data.

    • Example:

      java@Scope("request")
@Component
public class RequestScopedBean {
    private String requestData;

    public String getRequestData() {
        return requestData;
    }

    public void setRequestData(String requestData) {
        this.requestData = requestData;
    }
}

  3. Configuration:

    • The request scope can be used with beans defined using annotations like @Component or in XML configuration.

    • Example (XML):

      xml<bean id="myRequestBean" class="com.example.MyRequestBean" scope="request"/>

  4. Comparison with Other Scopes:

    • session: A bean instance is created and lives for the duration of an HTTP session.

    • application: A bean instance is shared across the entire ServletContext.

    • singleton: A single instance of the bean is created and shared across the entire application context.

    • prototype: A new instance of the bean is created every time it is requested.

results.question_plain: Which of the following Spring bean scopes is described as having a lifetime that matches a single HTTP request?

prompt.question: Which of the following statements are true about Spring AOP and its proxy mechanisms?
feedbacks: Correct, CGLIB creates a subclass of the target object, allowing it to intercept calls to public and protected methods. However, private methods cannot be intercepted because they are not visible to subclasses.

Correct, JDK dynamic proxies work by implementing the interfaces of the target object. Therefore, only methods declared in the interfaces can be intercepted. If the target object does not implement any interfaces, JDK proxies cannot be used.

Incorrect, CGLIB cannot intercept private method calls because private methods are not visible to subclasses. CGLIB works by creating a subclass of the target class, so it can only intercept public and protected methods.

Incorrect, Spring AOP does not intercept internal method calls within the same object. AOP proxies only intercept external method calls made through the proxy object. Internal calls bypass the proxy and directly invoke the target method.
prompt.explanation:
Spring AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) allows developers to define cross-cutting concerns, such as logging, security, or transaction management, in a modular way. Spring AOP uses proxies to apply aspects to target objects.

Key Features of Spring AOP:

  1. Proxy Mechanisms:

    • JDK Dynamic Proxies:

      • Used when the target object implements one or more interfaces.

      • Only methods declared in the interfaces can be intercepted.

      • Example:

        javapublic interface MyService {
    void performTask();
}

    • CGLIB Proxies:

      • Used when the target object does not implement any interfaces.

      • Works by creating a subclass of the target class.

      • Can intercept public and protected methods but not private methods.

  2. Limitations of Spring AOP:

    • Internal Method Calls: Spring AOP cannot intercept internal method calls within the same object. This is because internal calls bypass the proxy and directly invoke the target method.

    • Private Methods: Neither JDK proxies nor CGLIB proxies can intercept private methods.

  3. Use Cases for AOP:

    • Logging: Automatically log method calls and their parameters.

    • Security: Enforce security rules before executing methods.

    • Transactions: Manage transactions declaratively using @Transactional.

Comparison of JDK Proxies and CGLIB Proxies:

Feature	                JDK Proxies	                                CGLIB Proxies
Target Type	        Requires interfaces	                        Works with classes (no interfaces required)
Methods Intercepted	Public methods in interfaces	                Public and protected methods
Private Methods	        Not intercepted	Not intercepted
Performance	        Slightly faster for interface-based targets	Slightly slower due to subclass creation
answers: When using CGLIB proxies, public and protected method calls can be intercepted
When using JDK Proxies, only public interface method calls can be intercepted
When using CGLIB proxies, private method calls can be intercepted
Calls within the same object can be intercepted.

results.question_plain: Which of the following statements are true about Spring AOP and its proxy mechanisms?

prompt.question: Which of the following statements are true regarding Spring Boot’s build outputs?

Correct, Spring Boot applications can also be packaged as WAR files, but this requires additional configuration. WAR files are typically used when deploying applications to external servlet containers.

However, this requires explicitly changing the packaging type to war in the build configuration. By default, Spring Boot generates JAR files, even for web applications.
Correct, all the listed statements are true. Spring Boot supports both JAR and WAR outputs, and web applications can be configured to generate WAR files for deployment on standalone servers.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Boot provides flexibility in packaging applications as either executable JAR files or WAR files, depending on the deployment requirements.

Key Features of Spring Boot Build Outputs:

  1. Executable JAR Files (Default):

    • By default, Spring Boot applications are packaged as executable JAR files.

    • These JAR files include an embedded web server (e.g., Tomcat, Jetty, or Undertow) and can be run directly using the java -jar command.

    • Example (pom.xml):

      xml<packaging>jar</packaging>

  2. WAR Files (Optional):

    • Spring Boot applications can be packaged as WAR files if they need to be deployed to an external servlet container.

    • This requires changing the packaging type to war in the build configuration and extending the SpringBootServletInitializer class.

    • Example (pom.xml):

      xml<packaging>war</packaging>

    • Example (Java code):

      java@SpringBootApplication

      • Suitable for traditional application server environments.
answers: The build output file of Spring Boot can be a jar.

prompt.question: What happens if a method annotated with @Transactional calls another method within the same class that is also annotated with @Transactional?
feedbacks:
Correct, when methodA (annotated with @Transactional) calls methodB within the same class, the call bypasses the proxy mechanism. However, methodB executes within the transaction that was already started by methodA.
The @Transactional annotation on methodB is ignored (for example, it cannot start a new transaction with REQUIRES_NEW), but methodB still runs inside the existing transactional context.

Incorrect, Spring AOP does not intercept internal calls within the same class, so the second method will not execute in a new transaction. However, it will execute in the existing transaction started by the first method.

Incorrect, this statement is misleading. While the proxy mechanism is bypassed and the @Transactional annotation on the second method is ignored, the second method does execute within the transactional context that was already started by the first method. It doesn’t run outside of a transaction entirely.
Incorrect, Spring does not throw an exception in this scenario. The second method will execute within the existing transaction started by the first method.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, transaction management is implemented using AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming). When a method annotated with @Transactional is called, Spring creates a proxy that wraps the target object.
This proxy is responsible for managing the transactional context.

Internal Method Calls and Proxy Bypass

Internal method calls within the same class bypass the proxy mechanism because the method is called directly on the target object, not through the proxy.
As a result:

  • The @Transactional annotation on the second method is ignored (it cannot create a new transaction, change propagation, etc.).

  • However, the second method still executes within the transaction that was already started by the first method.

Example

@Service
public class MyService {

    @Transactional
    public void methodA() {
        // Transaction starts here
        methodB(); // Direct call, bypasses the proxy
    }

    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
    public void methodB() {
        // The @Transactional annotation is IGNORED
        // But methodB still runs in the transaction started by methodA
        // It will NOT create a new transaction despite REQUIRES_NEW
    }
}

Explanation:

  1. methodA() starts a transaction.

  2. When methodA() calls methodB(), the call bypasses the proxy.

  3. methodB() executes within the existing transaction from methodA.

  4. The @Transactional annotation on methodB (including REQUIRES_NEW) is ignored.

How to Fix This Issue

1. Refactor the Code

Move the second method (methodB) to a separate class, ensuring that the call goes through the proxy.

Example:

@Service
public class MyServiceB {

    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
    public void methodB() {
        // Now the @Transactional annotation is respected
        // A new transaction will be created
    }
}

2. Use Self-Injection

Inject the bean into itself and call through the proxy reference, not directly.

Example:

@Service
public class MyService {

    @Autowired
    private MyService self;

    @Transactional
    public void methodA() {
        self.methodB(); // Calls through proxy
    }

    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
    public void methodB() {
        // Now creates a new transaction
    }
}

By ensuring that transactional method calls go through the Spring proxy, you allow the @Transactional annotations to be properly applied and the intended transaction propagation behavior to take effect.
answers: The second method will execute within the same transaction as the first method.
The second method will execute in a new transaction, as it is annotated with @Transactional.
The second method will not execute in a transactional context, as the proxy mechanism is bypassed for internal calls.
Spring will throw an exception because nested transactions are not allowed by default.

prompt.question: What will happen if you use the CascadeType.REMOVE on a relationship in JPA and try to delete the parent entity?
feedbacks: Incorrect, CascadeType.REMOVE ensures that the child entities are also deleted when the parent entity is deleted.
Correct, CascadeType.REMOVE propagates the delete operation to all related child entities, ensuring that they are removed from the database along with the parent entity.
Incorrect, CascadeType.REMOVE does not set child entities to null. It deletes them from the database.
Incorrect, JPA supports cascading delete operations when CascadeType.REMOVE is specified.
prompt.explanation:
In JPA, the CascadeType.REMOVE is used to propagate the delete operation from a parent entity to its related child entities. This means that when the parent entity is deleted, all associated child entities are also deleted from the database.

Key Points About CascadeType.REMOVE:

  1. Propagation of Delete Operation:

    • When a parent entity is deleted, all child entities associated with it are also deleted.

    • This is useful for maintaining referential integrity in the database.

  2. Usage in Relationships:

    • CascadeType.REMOVE is commonly used in one-to-many and many-to-one relationships to ensure that child entities are removed when the parent entity is deleted.

    • Example:

      java@Entity
public class Parent {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue
    private Long id;

    @OneToMany(mappedBy = "parent", cascade = CascadeType.REMOVE)
    private List<Child> children;
}

@Entity
public class Child {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue
    private Long id;

    @ManyToOne
    private Parent parent;
}

  3. Behavior:

    • When the Parent entity is deleted, all Child entities associated with it are also deleted.

    • Example:

      javaParent parent = entityManager.find(Parent.class, 1L);
entityManager.remove(parent); // This will also delete all associated Child entities

  4. Important Considerations:

    • Use CascadeType.REMOVE cautiously, as it can lead to unintended data loss if not properly managed.

    • It is recommended to use cascading operations only when the lifecycle of child entities is tightly coupled with the parent entity.
answers: The parent entity will be deleted, but the child entities will remain in the database.
The parent entity and all related child entities will be deleted from the database.
The parent entity will be deleted, but the child entities will be set to null.
An exception will be thrown because JPA does not allow cascading delete operations.

prompt.question: Which one of the following properties can be used to expose all available Spring Boot Actuator endpoints?
feedbacks: Correct, this property is used to expose all actuator endpoints via HTTP by including them explicitly. By default, only a subset of endpoints is exposed.
Incorrect, this is not a valid property in Spring Boot for exposing actuator endpoints. The correct property is management.endpoints.web.exposure.include.
Incorrect, this property enables all actuator endpoints but does not expose them via HTTP. Endpoint exposure and enabling are separate configurations in Spring Boot.
Incorrect, this is not a valid property in Spring Boot. There is no such property for enabling or exposing actuator endpoints.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring Boot Actuator, actuator endpoints provide operational information about an application, such as health, metrics, and environment details. By default, only a subset of endpoints is exposed via HTTP for security reasons. To expose all available endpoints, the following property can be used:

propertiesmanagement.endpoints.web.exposure.include=*

This property explicitly includes all endpoints for HTTP exposure. Note that enabling an endpoint (making it functional) and exposing it (making it accessible via HTTP) are separate configurations. For example:

  • management.endpoints.enabled-by-default=true: Enables all endpoints but does not expose them via HTTP.

  • management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info: Exposes only the health and info endpoints via HTTP.

Practical Example:

# Enable all actuator endpoints
management.endpoints.enabled-by-default=true

# Expose all actuator endpoints via HTTP
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=*

This configuration ensures that all actuator endpoints are both enabled and exposed via HTTP.

In Spring Boot Actuator, actuator endpoints provide operational information about an application, such as health, metrics, and environment details.

By default, only a subset of endpoints is exposed via HTTP for security reasons.

Exposing All Endpoints

To expose all available endpoints, use the following property in your configuration file:

management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=*

This property explicitly includes all endpoints for HTTP exposure.

Enabling vs. Exposing Endpoints

It’s important to understand the difference between enabling and exposing endpoints:

  • management.endpoints.enabled-by-default=true
    Enables all endpoints, but does not automatically expose them via HTTP.
    (Most endpoints are already enabled by default.)

  • management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info
    Exposes only the health and info endpoints via HTTP.

Practical Example

To expose all Actuator endpoints via HTTP:

management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=*

Since most endpoints are already enabled by default, you typically only need to configure the exposure property to make them accessible via HTTP.
